1:
"Furthermore then we beseech you, brethren, and exhort you by the Lord Jesus, that as ye have received of us how ye ought to walk and to please God, so ye would abound more and more." The witnesses note Paul's tender exhortation to abound in pleasing God. Chrysostom notes that, having met what was pressing, Paul enters on things perpetual, adding "finally" (that is, always and forever); and, strange to say, he does not even speak of himself as of sufficient credit to exhort, but beseeches "in the Lord." The witnesses note Paul beseeching and exhorting the brethren, by the Lord Jesus, that as they had received how to walk and please God, so they would abound more and more; the humble plea to advance ever further in a God-pleasing life — that Paul, with the lowliness of one who entreats rather than commands (and that "by the Lord Jesus," in his name and authority), urges the Thessalonians not to rest content with the holy walk they had already learned and embraced, but to grow in it, abounding "more and more," so that the Christian life is set forth as a continual advance in pleasing God; abound more and more. The witnesses note we beseech you... and exhort you by the Lord Jesus. The witnesses note so ye would abound more and more.

The witnesses note Paul's humble plea — that the Thessalonians, having learned how to please God, would abound more and more; the Christian life a continual advance.

So the witnesses make the verse the exhortation to abound in pleasing God. The lesson the expositors draw is the call to advance ever further in a God-pleasing walk, not resting content with what was learned. The witnesses note the beseeching: "we beseech you, brethren, and exhort you by the Lord Jesus" — Chrysostom notes Paul beseeches in the Lord, not on his own credit; the beseeching; the exhort; the by the Lord Jesus; the lowly; the entreats. The expositors observe the received: "as ye have received of us how ye ought to walk and to please God" — they had received how to walk and please God; the received; the how to walk; the please God; the learned; the embraced. The witnesses press the abound: "so ye would abound more and more" — that they would abound more and more; the abound; the more and more; the grow; the advance; the increase. The expositors draw the lesson on the continual advance: the Christian life is a continual advance in pleasing God; the continual advance; the not rest content; the grow; the in pleasing God; the more. The witnesses observe the humility of the plea: Paul entreats rather than commands, in lowliness; the humility; the entreats; the not commands; the lowly; the by the Lord. The expositors note Chrysostom's note: Chrysostom notes Paul adds "finally," entering on perpetual things; the finally; the perpetual; the always; the forever; the enters. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the we beseech you and exhort you by the Lord Jesus, the so ye would abound more and more, and the call to advance. The verse thus records the exhortation to abound in pleasing God — teaching the call to advance ever further in a God-pleasing walk.
2:
"For ye know what commandments we gave you by the Lord Jesus." The witnesses note that the commandments came with the Lord's authority. Gill notes the commandments Paul gave when among them — concerning faith and love, the ordinances of the gospel, baptism and the Lord's supper, the worship of God, the discipline of Christ's house, their behaviour to one another and conduct in the world — were not delivered as from themselves, but by the Lord Jesus. The witnesses note Paul reminding them that they know what commandments he gave them by the Lord Jesus; the apostolic precepts carrying Christ's own authority — that Paul grounds his exhortation in the commandments they had already received from him, marking that these were not his own private rules but precepts given "by the Lord Jesus" (by his authority and in his name), so that to obey them was to obey Christ himself, and the holy walk he urged rested on Christ's own command; what commandments we gave you by the Lord Jesus. The witnesses note ye know what commandments we gave you. The witnesses note by the Lord Jesus.

The witnesses note that the commandments Paul gave came "by the Lord Jesus" — not his own private rules, but precepts carrying Christ's own authority.

So the witnesses make the verse the commandments by the Lord's authority. The lesson the expositors draw is that the apostolic precepts were Christ's own commands, so to obey them was to obey Christ. The witnesses note the commandments: "ye know what commandments we gave you" — Gill notes the commandments given when among them; the commandments; the gave you; the the precepts; the received; the known. The expositors observe the subject: the commandments concerned faith, love, the ordinances, worship, discipline, conduct; the subject; the faith; the love; the ordinances; the conduct. The witnesses press the by the Lord Jesus: "by the Lord Jesus" — given by the Lord Jesus (his authority and name); the by the Lord Jesus; the his authority; the his name; the not their own; the Christ's. The expositors draw the lesson on Christ's authority: the precepts carried Christ's own authority, not Paul's private rules; the Christ's authority; the not private rules; the carried; the Christ's; the command. The witnesses observe the obey Christ: to obey them was to obey Christ himself; the obey Christ; the to obey them; the Christ himself; the obedience; the his command. The expositors note the holy walk: the holy walk Paul urged rested on Christ's own command; the holy walk; the urged; the rested; the Christ's command; the grounded. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the ye know what commandments we gave you, the by the Lord Jesus, and the commandments by the Lord's authority. The verse thus records the commandments by the Lord's authority — teaching that the apostolic precepts were Christ's own commands, so to obey them was to obey Christ.
3:
"For this is the will of God, even your sanctification, that ye should abstain from fornication:" The witnesses note that sanctification is the will of God. Gill notes sanctification is internal or external; internal sanctification is the work of the Spirit, a principle of spiritual life in the soul, light in the understanding, the bending of the will to God's will; here especially holiness of life is meant, of which abstaining from fornication is a part. The witnesses note Paul declaring that this is the will of God, even their sanctification, that they abstain from fornication; the will of God for holiness, beginning with sexual purity — that Paul enforces his call to a God-pleasing walk by naming God's own will: that his people be sanctified, set apart and made holy, and he singles out as a first and chief instance the abstaining from "fornication" (all unlawful sexual intercourse), a sin to which the heathen world was wholly given but from which the holy God calls his people to keep clean; this is the will of God, even your sanctification. The witnesses note this is the will of God, even your sanctification. The witnesses note that ye should abstain from fornication.

The witnesses note that sanctification is the will of God — beginning with abstaining from fornication; the holy God calls his people to purity from the sin the heathen indulged.

So the witnesses make the verse God's will for holiness, beginning with sexual purity. The lesson the expositors draw is that God wills his people's sanctification, of which abstaining from fornication is a first and chief instance. The witnesses note the will of God: "this is the will of God, even your sanctification" — Gill notes sanctification, holiness of life, is God's will; the will of God; the sanctification; the holiness; the set apart; the made holy. The expositors observe the internal and external: sanctification is internal (the Spirit's work) and external (holiness of life); the internal; the external; the the Spirit's work; the holiness of life; the both. The witnesses press the abstain: "that ye should abstain from fornication" — abstain from fornication (all unlawful intercourse); the abstain; the fornication; the unlawful intercourse; the keep clean; the purity. The expositors draw the lesson on the first instance: abstaining from fornication is a first and chief instance of sanctification; the first instance; the chief; the fornication; the sanctification; the purity. The witnesses observe the heathen given to it: the heathen world was wholly given to this sin; the heathen; the given to it; the the world; the indulged; the unclean. The expositors note the holy God calls clean: the holy God calls his people to keep clean from it; the holy God; the calls; the keep clean; the his people; the purity. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the this is the will of God even your sanctification, the that ye should abstain from fornication, and the will for holiness. The verse thus records God's will for holiness, beginning with sexual purity — teaching that God wills his people's sanctification, of which abstaining from fornication is a chief instance.
4:
"That every one of you should know how to possess his vessel in sanctification and honour;" The witnesses note the call to possess one's vessel in sanctity and honour. Gill notes by "vessel" may be meant either a man's wife or his body, the Jews calling both by this name; either way, the believer is to possess it (govern, keep, or use it) in sanctification and honour. The witnesses note Paul charging that each should know how to possess his vessel in sanctification and honour; the holy government of body (or wife) in purity — that Paul, applying the call to purity, bids each believer learn to "possess his vessel" — whether his own body (keeping it pure and under control) or his wife (treating the marriage relation with chastity and respect) — and that "in sanctification" (holiness) and "honour" (with reverence and decency), the opposite of the shameful indulgence of lust; possess his vessel in sanctification and honour. The witnesses note know how to possess his vessel. The witnesses note in sanctification and honour.

The witnesses note the call to possess one's vessel — body or wife — in sanctification and honour, the opposite of the shameful indulgence of lust.

So the witnesses make the verse the holy government of the vessel. The lesson the expositors draw is that each believer is to keep his body (or wife) in holiness and honour, against the indulgence of lust. The witnesses note the vessel: "that every one of you should know how to possess his vessel" — Gill notes the "vessel" is the body or the wife; the vessel; the the body; the the wife; the possess; the govern. The expositors observe the possess: to possess (govern, keep, use) the vessel; the possess; the govern; the keep; the use; the master. The witnesses press the sanctification: "in sanctification" — in sanctification (holiness); the sanctification; the holiness; the pure; the holy; the clean. The expositors draw the lesson on the honour: "and honour" — and honour (with reverence and decency); the honour; the reverence; the decency; the respect; the dignified. The witnesses observe the body kept pure: if the body, it is to be kept pure and under control; the body; the kept pure; the under control; the chaste; the clean. The expositors note the wife with chastity: if the wife, the marriage relation is to be treated with chastity and respect; the wife; the marriage; the chastity; the respect; the honour. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the know how to possess his vessel, the in sanctification and honour, and the holy government. The verse thus records the holy government of the vessel — teaching that each believer is to keep his body (or wife) in holiness and honour, against the indulgence of lust.
5:
"Not in the lust of concupiscence, even as the Gentiles which know not God:" The witnesses note the contrast with the lustful heathen who know not God. Gill notes that to possess the vessel "not in the lust of concupiscence" is not to cherish the sin of concupiscence, the first motions of sin in the heart, nor to blow up the flame of lust, nor make provision for the flesh to fulfil its lusts. The witnesses note Paul charging that the vessel be possessed not in the lust of concupiscence, as the Gentiles who know not God; the warning against heathen-like indulgence of lust — that Paul sets the believer's holy use of the vessel against the very opposite: the unrestrained "lust of concupiscence" (the passionate gratifying of evil desire), which marks "the Gentiles which know not God," for ignorance of God and slavery to lust go together, the heathen, lacking the knowledge and fear of God, giving themselves up to sensuality, from which the believer is wholly to differ; not in the lust of concupiscence, as the Gentiles which know not God. The witnesses note not in the lust of concupiscence. The witnesses note even as the Gentiles which know not God.

The witnesses note the warning against heathen-like lust — the Gentiles who know not God give themselves to concupiscence, ignorance of God and slavery to lust going together.

So the witnesses make the verse the contrast with the lustful heathen. The lesson the expositors draw is that the believer's holy use of the vessel differs wholly from the unrestrained lust of those who know not God. The witnesses note the not in lust: "not in the lust of concupiscence" — Gill notes not cherishing concupiscence, not inflaming lust; the not in lust; the concupiscence; the not cherishing; the not inflaming; the restrained. The expositors observe the evil desire: the lust of concupiscence is the passionate gratifying of evil desire; the evil desire; the lust; the passionate; the gratifying; the unrestrained. The witnesses press the Gentiles: "even as the Gentiles" — as the Gentiles (heathen); the Gentiles; the heathen; the the pagans; the the world; the unconverted. The expositors draw the lesson on the know not God: "which know not God" — who know not God; the know not God; the ignorance; the without God; the no knowledge; the no fear. The witnesses observe the ignorance and lust together: ignorance of God and slavery to lust go together; the ignorance and lust; the together; the without God; the sensuality; the slavery. The expositors note the believer differs: the believer is wholly to differ from the heathen's sensuality; the believer differs; the wholly; the the heathen; the sensuality; the holy. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the not in the lust of concupiscence, the even as the Gentiles which know not God, and the contrast. The verse thus records the contrast with the lustful heathen — teaching that the believer's holy use of the vessel differs wholly from the unrestrained lust of those who know not God.
6:
"That no man go beyond and defraud his brother in any matter: because that the Lord is the avenger of all such, as we also have forewarned you and testified." The witnesses note the warning against wronging and defrauding others, with the Lord as avenger. Gill notes this is commonly understood of transgressing the bounds of justice and equity between men — cheating and defrauding in trade, by false prices, broken bargains, or covetous dealing — though some connect it still with the matter of impurity. The witnesses note Paul charging that no man go beyond or defraud his brother in any matter, since the Lord is the avenger of all such, as he had forewarned them; the warning against injustice, backed by God's avenging judgment — that Paul, whether still on the theme of impurity (wronging a brother in his marriage) or now of fraud in dealings, forbids any going beyond the bounds of right to overreach and defraud another, and enforces it with the solemn reminder that "the Lord is the avenger" of all such wrongs, who will himself punish the oppressor, a warning Paul had given before; the Lord is the avenger of all such. The witnesses note that no man go beyond and defraud his brother in any matter. The witnesses note the Lord is the avenger of all such.

The witnesses note the warning against defrauding others — the Lord being the avenger of all such wrongs, who will himself punish the oppressor.

So the witnesses make the verse the warning against injustice with God as avenger. The lesson the expositors draw is that wronging or defrauding another is forbidden, the Lord himself avenging all such wrongs. The witnesses note the go beyond: "that no man go beyond and defraud his brother in any matter" — Gill notes overreaching and defrauding, in impurity or in dealings; the go beyond; the defraud; the overreach; the in any matter; the wrong. The expositors observe the two readings: the witnesses note it may concern wronging a brother in his marriage, or fraud in trade and dealings; the two readings; the the marriage; the fraud; the dealings; the wrong. The witnesses press the avenger: "because that the Lord is the avenger of all such" — the Lord is the avenger of all such wrongs; the avenger; the the Lord; the all such; the punish; the judgment. The expositors draw the lesson on the divine judgment: God himself will punish the oppressor and defrauder; the divine judgment; the punish; the the oppressor; the defrauder; the avenged. The witnesses observe the forewarned: "as we also have forewarned you and testified" — Paul had forewarned them of this; the forewarned; the testified; the before; the warned; the solemn. The expositors note the solemn reminder: the warning is enforced with a solemn reminder of God's avenging; the solemn reminder; the enforced; the God's avenging; the warning; the judgment. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the that no man go beyond and defraud his brother in any matter, the the Lord is the avenger of all such, and the warning against injustice. The verse thus records the warning against injustice with God as avenger — teaching that wronging or defrauding another is forbidden, the Lord himself avenging all such wrongs.
7:
"For God hath not called us unto uncleanness, but unto holiness." The witnesses note the holy end of the believer's calling. Gill notes this is another reason against unclean practices, taken from the end of the effectual calling by the efficacious grace of God, which is not to uncleanness. Barnes notes that when God called us to be his followers, it was not that we should be impure. The witnesses note Paul declaring that God has not called us unto uncleanness, but unto holiness; the calling that aims at holiness, not impurity — that Paul presses his charge home by the very nature and design of the Christian calling: God, in calling his people by his grace, did not call them to a life of "uncleanness" (impurity and lust), but "unto holiness" (sanctity of heart and life), so that to live in impurity is to contradict the whole purpose of the calling, which was to make them holy; God hath not called us unto uncleanness, but unto holiness. The witnesses note God hath not called us unto uncleanness. The witnesses note but unto holiness.

The witnesses note the holy end of the calling — God called us not to uncleanness but to holiness, so impurity contradicts the whole purpose of the calling.

So the witnesses make the verse the holy end of the calling. The lesson the expositors draw is that the Christian calling aims at holiness, not impurity, so to live in uncleanness is to contradict it. The witnesses note the not unto uncleanness: "God hath not called us unto uncleanness" — Gill notes God did not call to uncleanness; the not uncleanness; the not impurity; the not called; the not lust; the holy. The expositors observe Barnes's note: Barnes notes God called us not to be impure; the not impure; the called; the not that; the his followers; the holy. The witnesses press the unto holiness: "but unto holiness" — but unto holiness (sanctity of heart and life); the unto holiness; the sanctity; the heart and life; the holy; the the aim. The expositors draw the lesson on the calling's design: the calling's design is holiness; the calling's design; the holiness; the purpose; the aim; the the end. The witnesses observe the contradicts the calling: to live in impurity contradicts the whole purpose of the calling; the contradicts; the impurity; the the purpose; the the calling; the opposed. The expositors note the made holy: the calling was to make them holy; the made holy; the the calling; the holy; the purpose; the design. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the God hath not called us unto uncleanness, the but unto holiness, and the holy end of the calling. The verse thus records the holy end of the calling — teaching that the Christian calling aims at holiness, not impurity, so to live in uncleanness is to contradict it.
8:
"He therefore that despiseth, despiseth not man, but God, who hath also given unto us his holy Spirit." The witnesses note that to despise these precepts is to despise God himself. Gill notes that he who rejects these exhortations with contempt — the commandments given by Christ and the will of God declared — despises not man, but God; for the precepts are not human but divine. The witnesses note Paul declaring that he who despises these things despises not man but God, who has given us his Holy Spirit; the divine weight of the precepts and the Spirit who sanctifies — that Paul clinches his charge to holiness by warning that whoever rejects and contemns these commands is not slighting a mere man (Paul or any teacher) but God himself, whose will and word they are; and he adds the further solemnity that God "hath given unto us his holy Spirit" — the very Spirit of holiness, whose presence both empowers the sanctity required and is grieved and affronted by the impurity forbidden; despiseth not man, but God, who hath given us his holy Spirit. The witnesses note he that despiseth, despiseth not man, but God. The witnesses note who hath also given unto us his holy Spirit.

The witnesses note that to despise these precepts is to despise God, not man — who has given us his Holy Spirit, the Spirit of holiness grieved by impurity.

So the witnesses make the verse the divine weight of the precepts and the sanctifying Spirit. The lesson the expositors draw is that to reject these commands is to despise God himself, who has given his Holy Spirit. The witnesses note the despiseth: "he therefore that despiseth" — Gill notes he who rejects these exhortations with contempt; the despiseth; the rejects; the contempt; the these things; the the precepts. The expositors observe the not man: "despiseth not man" — despises not man (Paul or any teacher); the not man; the not Paul; the not a teacher; the not human; the slighting. The witnesses press the but God: "but God" — but God himself, whose will and word they are; the but God; the God himself; the his will; the his word; the divine. The expositors draw the lesson on the divine precepts: the precepts are not human but divine; the divine precepts; the not human; the God's; the his will; the his word. The witnesses observe the given the Spirit: "who hath also given unto us his holy Spirit" — God has given us his Holy Spirit; the given the Spirit; the his holy Spirit; the the Spirit; the given; the indwelling. The expositors note the Spirit of holiness: the Spirit of holiness empowers the sanctity and is grieved by impurity; the Spirit of holiness; the empowers; the the sanctity; the grieved; the impurity. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the he that despiseth despiseth not man but God, the who hath also given unto us his holy Spirit, and the divine weight. The verse thus records the divine weight of the precepts and the sanctifying Spirit — teaching that to reject these commands is to despise God himself, who has given his Holy Spirit.
9:
"But as touching brotherly love ye need not that I write unto you: for ye yourselves are taught of God to love one another." The witnesses note the brotherly love taught of God. Chrysostom notes that Paul, about to discourse of brotherly love, introduces it as if passing it over — "ye need not that I write" — and this from his great wisdom, for praising them for what they had makes them more zealous than rebuking them for what they lacked. The witnesses note Paul saying that, as to brotherly love, they need not be written to, for they are taught of God to love one another; the love that God himself implants needing no further command — that Paul, turning from purity to love, pays the Thessalonians the high compliment of saying he need not write to them of brotherly love, since they "are taught of God" — that is, the Holy Spirit himself had inwardly taught them this grace, writing the law of love on their hearts — so that their love was not a mere lesson learned from men but a thing divinely wrought in them; ye yourselves are taught of God to love one another. The witnesses note ye need not that I write unto you. The witnesses note ye yourselves are taught of God to love one another.

The witnesses note the brotherly love taught of God — needing no further command, for the Spirit himself had written the law of love on their hearts.

So the witnesses make the verse the love taught of God. The lesson the expositors draw is that the Thessalonians' brotherly love was divinely wrought, the Spirit himself teaching them to love. The witnesses note the need not write: "as touching brotherly love ye need not that I write unto you" — Chrysostom notes Paul introduces it as if passing over; the need not write; the brotherly love; the passing over; the compliment; the praise. The expositors observe the praising them: Chrysostom notes praising them for what they had makes them more zealous; the praising; the for what they had; the more zealous; the than rebuking; the wisdom. The witnesses press the taught of God: "for ye yourselves are taught of God" — they are taught of God; the taught of God; the the Spirit; the inwardly; the divinely; the taught. The expositors draw the lesson on the law of love: the Spirit had written the law of love on their hearts; the law of love; the written; the the heart; the the Spirit; the implanted. The witnesses observe the to love one another: "to love one another" — to love one another; the love one another; the brotherly love; the mutual; the the grace; the love. The expositors note the not from men: their love was not a mere lesson from men, but divinely wrought; the not from men; the not a lesson; the divinely wrought; the the Spirit; the implanted. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the ye need not that I write unto you, the ye yourselves are taught of God to love one another, and the love taught of God. The verse thus records the love taught of God — teaching that the Thessalonians' brotherly love was divinely wrought, the Spirit himself teaching them to love.
10:
"And indeed ye do it toward all the brethren which are in all Macedonia: but we beseech you, brethren, that ye increase more and more;" The witnesses note their wide brotherly love and the call to increase. Gill notes they exercised this grace of love without respect of persons, toward all the brethren — high or low, rich or poor, bond or free — and not only those in the community at Thessalonica, but in all Macedonia. The witnesses note Paul observing that they did love toward all the brethren in all Macedonia, but beseeching them to increase more and more; the broad love commended yet urged to grow — that Paul, having praised their love as taught of God, adds proof of it — they showed it not narrowly but toward all the brethren throughout the whole province — yet, true to his pattern, he does not let even a real grace rest, but entreats them still to "increase more and more," for in love there is always room to grow; ye increase more and more. The witnesses note ye do it toward all the brethren which are in all Macedonia. The witnesses note that ye increase more and more.

The witnesses note their broad love to all the brethren in Macedonia, yet the call to increase more and more — in love there is always room to grow.

So the witnesses make the verse the broad love commended yet urged to grow. The lesson the expositors draw is that their wide love is praised, yet they are entreated still to increase in it. The witnesses note the toward all: "ye do it toward all the brethren which are in all Macedonia" — Gill notes they loved all the brethren throughout Macedonia; the toward all; the all the brethren; the all Macedonia; the broad; the wide. The expositors observe the without respect: they loved without respect of persons — high or low, rich or poor; the without respect; the high or low; the rich or poor; the all; the broad. The witnesses press the increase: "but we beseech you, brethren, that ye increase more and more" — but he beseeches them to increase more and more; the increase; the more and more; the grow; the entreats; the further. The expositors draw the lesson on the grace urged to grow: even a real grace is not let rest, but urged to grow; the urged to grow; the not let rest; the a real grace; the increase; the further. The witnesses observe the room to grow: in love there is always room to grow; the room to grow; the always; the in love; the further; the increase. The expositors note the proof of love: their wide love was proof of the grace taught of God; the proof; the wide love; the the grace; the taught of God; the shown. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the ye do it toward all the brethren which are in all Macedonia, the that ye increase more and more, and the love urged to grow. The verse thus records the broad love urged to grow — teaching that their wide love is praised, yet they are entreated still to increase in it.
11:
"And that ye study to be quiet, and to do your own business, and to work with your own hands, as we commanded you;" The witnesses note the charge to quiet, diligent, self-supporting living. Gill notes that to "study to be quiet" is to live peaceably in their families, to give no disturbance to others by talebearing or backbiting, to behave with quietness in their neighbourhood, and to lead a quiet and peaceable life in all godliness and honesty. The witnesses note Paul charging them to study to be quiet, to do their own business, and to work with their own hands, as he had commanded; the call to a peaceable, industrious, self-reliant life — that Paul, perhaps correcting an idle restlessness (occasioned by excitement over Christ's coming), bids the believers make it their ambition (to "study," earnestly endeavour) to live quietly, minding their own affairs rather than meddling in others', and labouring honestly with their own hands to support themselves, the very opposite of an idle, busybody, or disorderly walk; study to be quiet, and to do your own business, and to work with your own hands. The witnesses note study to be quiet, and to do your own business. The witnesses note to work with your own hands.

The witnesses note the charge to quiet, diligent, self-supporting living — studying to be quiet, minding one's own business, working with one's own hands.

So the witnesses make the verse the call to a peaceable, industrious life. The lesson the expositors draw is the charge to live quietly, mind one's own affairs, and labour to support oneself. The witnesses note the study to be quiet: "that ye study to be quiet" — Gill notes to live peaceably, giving no disturbance; the study to be quiet; the peaceably; the no disturbance; the no talebearing; the quiet. The expositors observe the study: "study" means earnestly endeavour, make it one's ambition; the study; the earnestly; the endeavour; the ambition; the aim. The witnesses press the own business: "and to do your own business" — minding their own affairs, not meddling; the own business; the own affairs; the not meddling; the not busybody; the mind. The expositors draw the lesson on the working: "and to work with your own hands, as we commanded you" — labouring honestly with their own hands; the working; the own hands; the labour; the support; the diligent. The witnesses observe the self-reliant: they were to support themselves by honest labour; the self-reliant; the support; the honest labour; the self-supporting; the industrious. The expositors note the correcting idleness: Paul perhaps corrects an idle restlessness over Christ's coming; the correcting idleness; the idle restlessness; the Christ's coming; the busybody; the disorderly. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the study to be quiet and to do your own business, the to work with your own hands, and the peaceable industrious life. The verse thus records the call to a peaceable, industrious life — teaching the charge to live quietly, mind one's own affairs, and labour to support oneself.
12:
"That ye may walk honestly toward them that are without, and that ye may have lack of nothing." The witnesses note the aim of quiet industry — a good witness and independence. Gill notes to "walk honestly" is to walk decently, in good credit and reputation, providing things honest for themselves and families, and honestly paying every man his own. The witnesses note Paul giving the aim — that they walk honestly toward those without, and lack nothing; the twofold benefit of diligent living — a good name and self-sufficiency — that Paul names the ends served by the quiet, working life: first, "that ye may walk honestly toward them that are without" — that their conduct might be honourable and reputable in the eyes of unbelievers, giving no occasion of reproach but rather a good witness — and second, "that ye may have lack of nothing" — that, supporting themselves by their labour, they might be independent and free from want, not a burden on others; walk honestly toward them that are without, and have lack of nothing. The witnesses note walk honestly toward them that are without. The witnesses note that ye may have lack of nothing.

The witnesses note the aim of quiet industry — an honourable witness toward unbelievers and freedom from want, not a burden on others.

So the witnesses make the verse the twofold benefit of diligent living. The lesson the expositors draw is that the quiet, working life secures a good name before unbelievers and independence from want. The witnesses note the walk honestly: "that ye may walk honestly toward them that are without" — Gill notes to walk decently, in good credit, before those without; the walk honestly; the decently; the good credit; the them without; the reputation. The expositors observe the unbelievers: their conduct should be honourable in the eyes of unbelievers; the unbelievers; the in their eyes; the honourable; the reputable; the without. The witnesses press the good witness: a good witness, giving no occasion of reproach; the good witness; the no reproach; the no occasion; the honourable; the credit. The expositors draw the lesson on the lack nothing: "that ye may have lack of nothing" — that they might lack nothing (be self-sufficient); the lack nothing; the self-sufficient; the free from want; the independent; the supported. The witnesses observe the not a burden: supporting themselves, they would not be a burden on others; the not a burden; the support themselves; the others; the independent; the free. The expositors note the twofold benefit: the quiet life secures a good name and freedom from want; the twofold benefit; the good name; the freedom from want; the witness; the independence. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the walk honestly toward them that are without, the that ye may have lack of nothing, and the twofold benefit. The verse thus records the twofold benefit of diligent living — teaching that the quiet, working life secures a good name before unbelievers and independence from want.
13:
"But I would not have you to be ignorant, brethren, concerning them which are asleep, that ye sorrow not, even as others which have no hope." The witnesses note the comfort against ignorant sorrow for the dead. Chrysostom notes that many things from ignorance alone cause us sorrow, so that if we understand them well, we banish our grief; this Paul shows, saying "I would not have you ignorant, that ye sorrow not, even as the rest which have no hope." The witnesses note Paul declaring that he would not have them ignorant concerning those who are asleep, that they sorrow not as others who have no hope; the cure of grief in the knowledge of the believing dead — that Paul, turning to console the bereaved, names death gently as a "sleep" (for the believer's death is but a sleep from which he will surely wake), and would dispel their ignorance, the root of their grief; not that Christians may not mourn at all, but that they may not sorrow "as others which have no hope" — the heathen, who, knowing nothing of resurrection, grieved without comfort — for the believer's hope wholly changes the character of his mourning; that ye sorrow not, even as others which have no hope. The witnesses note concerning them which are asleep. The witnesses note that ye sorrow not, even as others which have no hope.

The witnesses note the comfort against ignorant grief — death is but a sleep, and believers are not to sorrow as the hopeless heathen, for hope changes their mourning.

So the witnesses make the verse the cure of grief in the knowledge of the believing dead. The lesson the expositors draw is that believers, knowing the dead but sleep, are not to sorrow as the hopeless. The witnesses note the not ignorant: "I would not have you to be ignorant... concerning them which are asleep" — Chrysostom notes ignorance causes sorrow; Paul dispels it; the not ignorant; the asleep; the ignorance; the the root; the grief. The expositors observe the asleep: death is named gently as "sleep" — the believer will surely wake; the asleep; the sleep; the gently; the surely wake; the death. The witnesses press the sorrow not: "that ye sorrow not" — that they sorrow not (immoderately); the sorrow not; the not immoderate; the grief; the mourn; the comforted. The expositors draw the lesson on the hopeless: "even as others which have no hope" — not as others (heathen) who have no hope; the hopeless; the the heathen; the no hope; the no resurrection; the without comfort. The witnesses observe the not no mourning: not that Christians may not mourn at all, but not as the hopeless; the not no mourning; the may mourn; the not as hopeless; the the character; the changed. The expositors note the hope changes grief: the believer's hope wholly changes the character of his mourning; the hope changes; the the character; the his mourning; the comforted; the changed. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the concerning them which are asleep, the that ye sorrow not even as others which have no hope, and the comfort against grief. The verse thus records the cure of grief in the knowledge of the believing dead — teaching that believers, knowing the dead but sleep, are not to sorrow as the hopeless.
14:
"For if we believe that Jesus died and rose again, even so them also which sleep in Jesus will God bring with him." The witnesses note the resurrection of Christ as the ground of the believing dead's hope. Gill notes that both the death and resurrection of Christ are fundamental articles of faith — nothing more certain or comforting than that Christ died for the sins of his people and rose again for their justification, on which the saints' present peace depends. The witnesses note Paul reasoning that, if we believe Jesus died and rose again, even so those who sleep in Jesus will God bring with him; the risen Christ the pledge of his sleeping people's resurrection — that Paul grounds the comfort in the gospel's heart: since it is the settled faith of all Christians that Jesus truly died and rose again, the same certainty assures us that "them also which sleep in Jesus" (those who died in faith and union with him) "will God bring with him" — that is, God will raise them and bring them in Christ's train at his coming, so that the resurrection of the Head is the sure guarantee of the resurrection of the members; them also which sleep in Jesus will God bring with him. The witnesses note if we believe that Jesus died and rose again. The witnesses note them also which sleep in Jesus will God bring with him.

The witnesses note the risen Christ as the pledge of his people's resurrection — since Jesus died and rose, God will bring those who sleep in Jesus with him.

So the witnesses make the verse the risen Christ the ground of hope. The lesson the expositors draw is that the resurrection of the Head guarantees the resurrection of the members who sleep in him. The witnesses note the believe: "if we believe that Jesus died and rose again" — Gill notes Christ's death and resurrection are fundamental articles; the believe; the Jesus died; the rose again; the the settled faith; the certain. The expositors observe the gospel's heart: the death and resurrection of Christ are the gospel's heart; the gospel's heart; the death and resurrection; the certain; the comforting; the foundation. The witnesses press the sleep in Jesus: "even so them also which sleep in Jesus" — those who died in faith and union with him; the sleep in Jesus; the died in faith; the union; the the dead; the asleep. The expositors draw the lesson on God bring with him: "will God bring with him" — God will raise them and bring them in Christ's train at his coming; the God bring; the raise; the in his train; the his coming; the bring. The witnesses observe the Head and members: the resurrection of the Head guarantees that of the members; the Head and members; the the Head; the the members; the guarantees; the resurrection. The expositors note the sure guarantee: the risen Christ is the sure pledge of his sleeping people's resurrection; the sure guarantee; the the risen Christ; the the pledge; the resurrection; the assured. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the if we believe that Jesus died and rose again, the them also which sleep in Jesus will God bring with him, and the risen Christ the ground of hope. The verse thus records the risen Christ the ground of hope — teaching that the resurrection of the Head guarantees the resurrection of the members who sleep in him.
15:
"For this we say unto you by the word of the Lord, that we which are alive and remain unto the coming of the Lord shall not prevent them which are asleep." The witnesses note that the living at Christ's coming shall not precede the dead. Chrysostom notes that the prophets, to show the credibility of their words, said "the vision which Isaiah saw" or "thus saith the Lord"; so Paul, speaking of a matter beyond human knowledge, grounds it "by the word of the Lord," a divine revelation. The witnesses note Paul declaring, by the word of the Lord, that those alive at Christ's coming shall not prevent (precede) those who are asleep; the divine assurance that the living shall not outstrip the dead — that Paul, to settle a question only God could answer, speaks "by the word of the Lord" (by direct revelation, not his own opinion), and assures the bereaved that the believers found living at Christ's coming will have no advantage over the dead, will not go before them into glory, for the sleeping saints shall be raised and share fully in the great reunion, losing nothing by having died first; we which are alive... shall not prevent them which are asleep. The witnesses note by the word of the Lord. The witnesses note shall not prevent them which are asleep.

The witnesses note the divine assurance — by the word of the Lord — that the living at Christ's coming shall not precede the dead, who lose nothing by having died first.

So the witnesses make the verse the assurance that the living shall not outstrip the dead. The lesson the expositors draw is that, by the word of the Lord, the believing dead lose no advantage, sharing fully in the reunion at Christ's coming. The witnesses note the word of the Lord: "this we say unto you by the word of the Lord" — Chrysostom notes Paul grounds it in divine revelation; the word of the Lord; the revelation; the not opinion; the divine; the grounded. The expositors observe the beyond human knowledge: this is a matter only God could answer; the beyond human knowledge; the only God; the revelation; the the word; the divine. The witnesses press the alive and remain: "that we which are alive and remain unto the coming of the Lord" — those alive at Christ's coming; the alive and remain; the at his coming; the the living; the found; the remaining. The expositors draw the lesson on shall not prevent: "shall not prevent them which are asleep" — shall not prevent (precede, go before) the dead; the shall not prevent; the not precede; the not go before; the the dead; the no advantage. The witnesses observe the no advantage: the living have no advantage over the dead; the no advantage; the the living; the over the dead; the equal; the share. The expositors note the lose nothing: the sleeping saints lose nothing by having died first; the lose nothing; the the dead; the died first; the share fully; the reunion. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the by the word of the Lord, the shall not prevent them which are asleep, and the assurance. The verse thus records the assurance that the living shall not outstrip the dead — teaching that, by the word of the Lord, the believing dead lose no advantage at Christ's coming.
16:
"For the Lord himself shall descend from heaven with a shout, with the voice of the archangel, and with the trump of God: and the dead in Christ shall rise first:" The witnesses note the glorious descent of the Lord and the first resurrection of the dead in Christ. Gill notes the Lord shall descend not by proxy, nor by the ministry of angels, nor by his Spirit, but in person, in his human nature, in soul and body, visibly and gloriously, from heaven where he now is. The witnesses note Paul declaring that the Lord himself shall descend from heaven with a shout, the voice of the archangel, and the trump of God, and the dead in Christ shall rise first; the majestic coming of Christ and the resurrection of his sleeping people — that Paul paints the scene of the second advent: the Lord "himself" (not by deputy) descending in person from heaven, attended by a "shout" (a cry of command), "the voice of the archangel," and "the trump of God" — the great signals summoning the dead — at which "the dead in Christ" (the believing departed) "shall rise first," raised before the living are changed, so that the sleeping saints are first cared for; the Lord himself shall descend from heaven... and the dead in Christ shall rise first. The witnesses note the Lord himself shall descend from heaven with a shout. The witnesses note the dead in Christ shall rise first.

The witnesses note the Lord's glorious descent — with a shout, the archangel's voice, the trump of God — at which the dead in Christ shall rise first, before the living are changed.

So the witnesses make the verse the glorious descent and the first resurrection. The lesson the expositors draw is that the Lord himself shall descend in majesty, and the believing dead shall rise first. The witnesses note the descent: "the Lord himself shall descend from heaven" — Gill notes the Lord descends in person, not by proxy; the descent; the the Lord himself; the in person; the not by proxy; the from heaven. The expositors observe the in person: he descends in his human nature, visibly and gloriously; the in person; the human nature; the visibly; the gloriously; the himself. The witnesses press the signals: "with a shout, with the voice of the archangel, and with the trump of God" — the shout, the archangel's voice, the trump of God; the signals; the shout; the the archangel; the the trump; the summoning. The expositors draw the lesson on the dead rise first: "and the dead in Christ shall rise first" — the believing dead shall rise first; the dead rise first; the the dead in Christ; the believing; the rise; the first. The witnesses observe the before the living: the dead are raised before the living are changed; the before the living; the raised; the the living changed; the first; the order. The expositors note the sleeping saints cared for: the sleeping saints are first cared for, not forgotten; the sleeping saints; the first cared for; the not forgotten; the raised; the order. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the the Lord himself shall descend from heaven with a shout, the the dead in Christ shall rise first, and the glorious descent. The verse thus records the glorious descent and the first resurrection — teaching that the Lord himself shall descend in majesty, and the believing dead shall rise first.
17:
"Then we which are alive and remain shall be caught up together with them in the clouds, to meet the Lord in the air: and so shall we ever be with the Lord." The witnesses note the catching up of the living with the risen dead to meet the Lord. Barnes notes the word "then" marks succession in time — afterwards, next in the order of events; after the dead are raised, the living shall be caught up. The witnesses note Paul declaring that then those alive and remaining shall be caught up together with the risen dead in the clouds, to meet the Lord in the air, and so be ever with him; the great reunion and the everlasting presence with the Lord — that Paul completes the scene: after the dead in Christ are raised, the believers then living shall be "caught up" (suddenly, by divine power, snatched away) "together with them" (the raised dead and the living united in one company) "in the clouds, to meet the Lord in the air," welcoming their descending King; and he crowns all with the sweetest comfort of the whole passage — "so shall we ever be with the Lord" — the saints, dead and living, gathered forever into the unbroken, eternal presence of Christ; caught up together with them... and so shall we ever be with the Lord. The witnesses note caught up together with them in the clouds, to meet the Lord in the air. The witnesses note so shall we ever be with the Lord.

The witnesses note the catching up of dead and living together to meet the Lord in the air — crowned with the sweetest comfort: "so shall we ever be with the Lord."

So the witnesses make the verse the great reunion and everlasting presence. The lesson the expositors draw is that the raised dead and living are caught up together to meet the Lord and to be with him forever. The witnesses note the then: "then we which are alive and remain" — Barnes notes "then" marks the order — after the dead are raised; the then; the order; the after; the the living; the succession. The expositors observe the caught up: "shall be caught up together with them" — caught up (snatched away) together with the risen dead; the caught up; the snatched away; the together; the the risen dead; the united. The witnesses press the to meet the Lord: "in the clouds, to meet the Lord in the air" — in the clouds, to meet the Lord in the air; the to meet the Lord; the in the clouds; the in the air; the welcome; the King. The expositors draw the lesson on the reunion: the raised dead and living are united in one company; the reunion; the united; the one company; the dead and living; the gathered. The witnesses observe the ever with the Lord: "and so shall we ever be with the Lord" — and so be ever with the Lord; the ever with the Lord; the forever; the the presence; the eternal; the with Christ. The expositors note the sweetest comfort: this is the sweetest comfort of the passage — eternal presence with Christ; the sweetest comfort; the the passage; the eternal presence; the with Christ; the crown. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the caught up together with them in the clouds to meet the Lord in the air, the so shall we ever be with the Lord, and the great reunion. The verse thus records the great reunion and everlasting presence — teaching that the raised dead and living are caught up together to meet the Lord and to be with him forever.
18:
"Wherefore comfort one another with these words." The witnesses note the practical use — mutual comfort from the doctrine of the coming. Gill notes they were to comfort one another with these words, or doctrines — that the saints, when they die, do not cease to be, but sleep in Jesus; that their souls are with him and their bodies his care; that they shall be raised and caught up to be with Christ; that his coming will be with power and glory. The witnesses note Paul charging them to comfort one another with these words; the doctrine of the coming given for the comfort of the bereaved — that Paul shows the great end of all he has revealed about the coming of the Lord and the resurrection: it is not for idle speculation about times and manner, but "wherefore comfort one another with these words" — that believers, and especially the bereaved, might console and strengthen each other with these truths, so that the doctrine of Christ's return is, above all, a fountain of comfort for those who mourn their sleeping saints; comfort one another with these words. The witnesses note wherefore comfort one another. The witnesses note with these words.

The witnesses note the practical end — comfort one another with these words; the doctrine of Christ's coming given, above all, as a fountain of comfort for the bereaved.

So the witnesses make the verse the comfort drawn from the doctrine of the coming. The lesson the expositors draw is that the truth of Christ's return is given chiefly for the mutual comfort of believers, especially the bereaved. The witnesses note the comfort: "wherefore comfort one another" — Gill notes they were to comfort one another; the comfort; the one another; the console; the strengthen; the mutual. The expositors observe the these words: "with these words" — with these words (doctrines); the these words; the doctrines; the the truths; the the coming; the revealed. The witnesses press the the saints sleep: Gill notes the comfort — the saints sleep in Jesus, their souls with him; the the saints sleep; the in Jesus; the souls with him; the bodies his care; the comfort. The expositors draw the lesson on the not speculation: the doctrine is not for idle speculation but for comfort; the not speculation; the not times and manner; the for comfort; the practical; the end. The witnesses observe the fountain of comfort: the doctrine of Christ's return is a fountain of comfort for the bereaved; the fountain of comfort; the the bereaved; the the return; the console; the mourn. The expositors note the mutual strengthening: believers are to console and strengthen each other; the mutual strengthening; the console; the strengthen; the each other; the comfort. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the wherefore comfort one another, the with these words, and the comfort drawn from the doctrine. The verse thus records the comfort drawn from the doctrine of the coming — teaching that the truth of Christ's return is given chiefly for the mutual comfort of believers, especially the bereaved.
